skill.name: directory-submissions
skill.description: Plan and manage directory submissions for software launches, including readiness checks, tier selection, positioning variants, backlink verification, and submission tracking.
skill.summary: Build a directory submission plan for launch backlinks, discovery, reviews, and AI citation visibility.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity keyword hits, but the weak-crypto, scheduling, SAM, and critical-combination signals are false positives caused by markdown text, timestamps, SAML, and directory content. Real risks are moderate: the skill instructs local product-marketing file reads, provides curl-based backlink verification, and includes many third-party submission URLs where users may share product or account data.

medium_findings.title: External Network Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends curl and grep commands to verify backlink headers. This is a true positive for external command and network activity because an agent could make requests to user-supplied listing URLs, although the purpose is backlink verification rather than execution or exfiltration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command pattern is present in the skill instructions and evaluation expectations. Confidence is below very high because it is a benign verification workflow, not evidence of malicious command execution.

medium_findings.title: Local Product Marketing Context Read
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read local product-marketing files before asking questions. This can be legitimate for personalization, but those files may contain confidential strategy, positioning, customer, or launch information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-read instruction is explicit and semantically relevant to the workflow. Risk is moderate because no automatic external transmission is shown, but the data could be used in third-party submission copy.

medium_findings.title: Large Third-Party Submission Surface
medium_findings.description: The tracker contains many external directory URLs for product submissions. This is expected for the skill, but users may enter product, founder, account email, and launch data into external services with different privacy and moderation policies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CSV is a direct catalog of third-party submission destinations. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because directory submission is the declared purpose of the skill.

low_findings.title: Paste Site Listed as a Profile Platform
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Pastebin as a data-exfiltration site. The context is a profile backlink row, not secret upload logic, but paste sites are easy to misuse and should not receive proprietary code, credentials, or customer data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Pastebin reference is real, but the surrounding text does not instruct users to paste secrets or automate uploads. It remains a low contextual risk because of the service type.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography, scheduling, reconnaissance, and Windows SAM findings are false positives. Reviewed lines contain marketing text, AM launch timing, directory names, SAML as a tag, markdown placeholders, or evaluation text, with no cryptographic API use, scheduler invocation, system enumeration, or SAM database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found benign markdown and content examples at representative flagged locations. Confidence is high because there are no executable code files or instructions matching the scanner labels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Pipeline for URL Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: Inline curl and grep examples can trigger outbound network requests when followed by an agent. Prefer manual inspection or require explicit user approval before running network commands against listing URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell pipeline is explicitly documented. It is dangerous only in the limited sense that it performs network I/O, not because it is obfuscated or malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Implicit Local Context File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs agents to read local product-marketing context files if present. Agents should treat that content as private user data and avoid including sensitive details in external directory copy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local file access instruction is clear. The risk depends on the contents of those files and how generated directory submissions are used.

content.value_statement: Software launches often waste time on low-quality directory submissions and repeated copy. This skill builds a prioritized submission plan with readiness checks, positioning variants, and tracking guidance.
actual_capabilities: Runs a launch readiness assessment before recommending directory submissions.
Prioritizes directories by launch tier, product category, and timing.
Generates positioning variants for startup, SaaS, AI, MCP, no-code, developer, and review platforms.
Builds Product Hunt preparation timelines and launch-day workflows.
Plans G2, Capterra, and TrustRadius review collection using a structured review drive.
Uses a tracker template to record submission status, live URLs, backlink checks, and notes.
limitations: Directory quality, domain rating, and submission rules can change after the reference files are written.
The skill does not submit forms or create accounts automatically.
Backlink verification may require manual checks or approved network commands.
Review platform guidance must be checked against current platform terms before incentives are offered.

use_cases.title: Launch a New SaaS Product
use_cases.description: Create a three-week directory plan that covers Product Hunt, startup directories, SaaS listings, and review platforms.

use_cases.title: Adapt Listings for AI Tools
use_cases.description: Prepare distinct descriptions and tags for AI directories, MCP registries, agent directories, and general software platforms.

use_cases.title: Build a Backlink Operations Tracker
use_cases.description: Track submission status, listing URLs, backlink verification, positioning variants, and follow-up notes across many directories.

prompt_templates.title: Check Launch Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess whether my product is ready for directory submissions. Ask for any missing launch assets, destination pages, pricing, policies, screenshots, and review capacity.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Directory Tiers
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a directory submission plan for my product. Include the tiers to use, tiers to skip, submission order, and why each choice fits my category.

prompt_templates.title: Create Positioning Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Create distinct directory listing copy for startup, SaaS, AI, MCP, no-code, developer, and B2B review platforms. Avoid duplicate long descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Post-Submission Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my completed directory tracker and tell me what to verify next. Include backlink checks, review collection, destination pages, and weekly KPI priorities.

output_examples.input: We are launching an AI SaaS in three weeks and need directory submissions.
output: A readiness checklist with hard blockers and soft blockers.
A week-by-week tier plan covering Product Hunt, SaaS directories, AI directories, and MCP registries when relevant.
A Product Hunt timeline with preparation, launch-day actions, and post-launch follow-up.

output_examples.input: Can we reuse the same description everywhere?
output: A clear recommendation to avoid duplicate descriptions.
Different opening angles for startup, SaaS, AI, MCP, no-code, developer, and review platforms.
A list of required assets for each positioning variant.

output_examples.input: We submitted to 50 directories last week. What now?
output: Next steps for alternative pages, use-case pages, review collection, and community distribution.
Weekly KPIs to track before submitting to more directories.

best_practices: Build destination pages before submitting so backlinks point to pages that can convert.
Use different positioning for each directory type and avoid duplicate long descriptions.
Track every submission, live URL, backlink status, account email, and moderator note.
anti_patterns: Submitting to directories before pricing, policies, screenshots, and landing pages are ready.
Paying mass-submission services that place products in low-quality or irrelevant directories.
Treating launch-day directory traffic as the whole growth strategy.

faq.question: Does this skill submit directories automatically?
faq.answer: No. It helps plan submissions, write variants, and track status. Users still complete forms and account steps manually.

faq.question: Which products fit this skill best?
faq.answer: It fits software, SaaS, AI tools, no-code products, MCP servers, agent tools, and launch-ready startup products.

faq.question: Why does it require readiness checks first?
faq.answer: Many directories reject incomplete products. Readiness checks reduce wasted submissions and help backlinks point to useful pages.

faq.question: Can I use one description for every directory?
faq.answer: No. The skill recommends different copy for each directory type because audiences and moderation standards differ.

faq.question: Does it cover Product Hunt?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Product Hunt preparation, launch-day timing, comment strategy, and post-launch follow-up.

faq.question: Does it handle review sites like G2 and Capterra?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when to list, how many reviews matter, and how to plan a compliant review drive.
